title: Systemd Timer Missed Triggers
spl:
index=os sourcetype=syslog "systemd" "timer" ("cannot run" OR "Skipping")
| stats count by host, timer_unit
| where count>0
description: Finds **systemd** timer messages that say a trigger could not run or was skipped, grouped by **host** and **timer_unit** when parsed.
value: Missed backups, **SLA** batch jobs, and security **rotate** tasks often first show up as timer journal lines, not as an app-level error code.
implementation: Some distros log these at **notice** not **err**—verify your **syslog** facility filter is not dropping them. Add `| where count>1` to avoid one-off clock skew noise.
knownFalsePositives: Hosts that sleep or **hibernate**; **DST** jumps; **timer** units that intentionally **skip** overlaps—read the specific **systemd** wording in `_raw` before paging.

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux

detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Timers defined in `/etc/systemd/system` or **vendor** trees, with **Persistent=true** where you need catch-up after downtime.


Step 3 — Validate
`systemctl list-timers --all` and `journalctl -u mytimer.timer` on the host; compare **NEXT** / **LEFT** with the Splunk timestamp.

Step 4 — Operationalize
When a timer is **missed**, check **OnCalendar=** vs actual host load first, not only the app.
grandmaExplanation: We help you know when a scheduled “do this later” task on the machine did not run when it should, which can quietly break backups or clean-up jobs.
